explanation: Equipment for animals use. Horse bridle

discriminators: EQUIPMENT is defined by USE, not by material or by morphology. Every subcategory here is the same physical event — a person handling worn or carried gear — producing a stochastic HANDLING TEXTURE: fabric rustle (broadband, high-centroid, noise-like) + leather creak (friction) + buckle and clasp jingle (sparse, bright, ringing impulses) + strap impacts. DSP can identify that texture precisely; it cannot tell whether the person wearing it is a soldier, an athlete, a hiker, or a horse. All six are honestly semantic_only.

The most MATERIALLY distinctive of the six, and the only one with a real acoustic lean. Bridles and tack are LEATHER AND METAL and nothing else. A bit, a curb chain, a stirrup and a buckle are small, hard, RINGING metal — sparse bright transients with a long metallic ring (high inharmonicity, long decay_time_seconds_60db, high spectral centroid) — over a LEATHER CREAK, which is a friction morphology: sustained, unstable, high-centroid contact. NO nylon, NO synthetic fabric.

So the profile is: metal jingle + leather creak, with essentially no low-frequency content. That genuinely distinguishes it from EQUIPRec (nylon: noise-like, no ring) and EQUIPSprt (padding: dull thuds, no ring).

It becomes RHYTHMIC when the animal moves: a walking horse imposes a gait period of ~0.5-1 Hz on the jingle, lifting onset_periodicity. Paired with hoof falls it is unmistakable in CONTEXT — but the context is what tells you, not the signal.
Against CHAINS, LETHRCreak, OBJJewl and METLMvmt: physically the SAME SOUNDS. UCS routes by USE (equipment for animals), not by material, so text must carry the call entirely.

explanation: complex suits with lots of gizmos or complex parts.

discriminators: EQUIPMENT is defined by USE, not by material or by morphology. Every subcategory here is the same physical event — a person handling worn or carried gear — producing a stochastic HANDLING TEXTURE: fabric rustle (broadband, high-centroid, noise-like) + leather creak (friction) + buckle and clasp jingle (sparse, bright, ringing impulses) + strap impacts. DSP can identify that texture precisely; it cannot tell whether the person wearing it is a soldier, an athlete, a hiker, or a horse. All six are honestly semantic_only.

Like MACHTech, this subcategory has NO REAL-WORLD REFERENT: spacesuits and 'complex suits with lots of gizmos' are DESIGNED, not recorded. Their feature vectors are whatever a sound designer chose — typically a layered assembly of servo whines (MOTRSrvo), pneumatic hisses, latch clicks and synthetic tonal beds, laid over an ordinary cloth-handling texture.
Consequently no gate and no tight prior is defensible; the priors below are broad and low-weight by intent, and exist only to say 'this is a busy, bright, layered, transient-rich object'.

The ONE thing that reliably separates EQUIPTech from its EQUIPMENT siblings is real: the presence of ELECTROMECHANICAL or SYNTHETIC layers — a servo whine, a tonal element, a beep — sitting on top of the handling texture. Real gear (tactical, sport, recreational, tack) has NO MOTOR IN IT and no tonal component at all. Harmonicity above ~0.5 in an EQUIPMENT file effectively means EQUIPTech.
Against SCIFI and MACHTech: the same design language, and untestable from signal. If it is WORN, it is here; if it is a MACHINE standing in the room, it is MACHTech or SCIMach.

explanation: Equipment sounds not fitting another category in this list. See also TOOLS and OBJECT categories.

discriminators: EQUIPMENT is defined by USE, not by material or by morphology. Every subcategory here is the same physical event — a person handling worn or carried gear — producing a stochastic HANDLING TEXTURE: fabric rustle (broadband, high-centroid, noise-like) + leather creak (friction) + buckle and clasp jingle (sparse, bright, ringing impulses) + strap impacts. DSP can identify that texture precisely; it cannot tell whether the person wearing it is a soldier, an athlete, a hiker, or a horse. All six are honestly semantic_only.

The EQUIPMENT catch-all. Un-gated so it stays reachable as the category's abstention target — and abstention is ESPECIALLY likely here, because the five siblings are acoustically near-identical and text is the only thing separating them. When the filename gives no use-context, MISC is the CORRECT answer, not a cop-out.
UCS points outward: 'See also TOOLS and OBJECT categories.' If the item is USED rather than WORN or CARRIED, it probably belongs there instead.

explanation: camping tents. Specifically outdoor recreation. See also OBJECTS-GYM.

discriminators: EQUIPMENT is defined by USE, not by material or by morphology. Every subcategory here is the same physical event — a person handling worn or carried gear — producing a stochastic HANDLING TEXTURE: fabric rustle (broadband, high-centroid, noise-like) + leather creak (friction) + buckle and clasp jingle (sparse, bright, ringing impulses) + strap impacts. DSP can identify that texture precisely; it cannot tell whether the person wearing it is a soldier, an athlete, a hiker, or a horse. All six are honestly semantic_only.

The NYLON subcategory. Backpacks, tents, sleeping bags, climbing harnesses and rain jackets are all SYNTHETIC FABRIC, so the dominant sound is a BRIGHT, NOISE-LIKE RUSTLE: high spectral_flatness, very high spectral_centroid (4 kHz+), high zero_crossings, essentially NO low-frequency energy, and NO RING AT ALL. It is closer to a texture of hiss than to a sequence of events — which is a genuine, measurable contrast with EQUIPBridle.

Accents: buckle clacks and ZIPPER runs. A zipper is worth naming — it is a fast, remarkably REGULAR impulse train (onset rates of 50-200/s) and one of the most identifiable objects in the whole ontology. UCS gives it its own home at OBJZipr.
Against EQUIPBridle: nylon DOES NOT RING. inharmonicity and decay_time_seconds_60db are LOW here and HIGH there. This is the one intra-EQUIPMENT boundary with real acoustic content.
Against EQUIPSprt and EQUIPTact: acoustically the SAME nylon and webbing. UCS's split — 'specifically outdoor recreation' versus sport versus military — is pure use-context and invisible to DSP.
Against CLOTHHndl and CLOTHMvmt: identical physics. The GEAR versus GARMENT boundary is semantic. UCS cross-references OBJECTS-GYM.

EQUIPMENT is defined by USE, not by material or by morphology. Every subcategory here is the same physical event — a person handling worn or carried gear — producing a stochastic HANDLING TEXTURE: fabric rustle (broadband, high-centroid, noise-like) + leather creak (friction) + buckle and clasp jingle (sparse, bright, ringing impulses) + strap impacts. DSP can identify that texture precisely; it cannot tell whether the person wearing it is a soldier, an athlete, a hiker, or a horse. All six are honestly semantic_only.

The PADDING subcategory. Shoulder pads, helmets, gloves, shin guards: the characteristic event is a DULL, DAMPED IMPACT. Foam and hard plastic ABSORB energy, so a pad slap is a high-crest transient with a SHORT decay and NO RING (decay_time_seconds_60db under ~80 ms, low inharmonicity), carrying more low-mid energy and a darker spectrum than any nylon rustle.

So the honest EQUIPSprt lean is: impacts that THUD, rather than RING (EQUIPBridle) or HISS (EQUIPRec). That is a genuine material difference — but it is nowhere near enough to gate on, and it is USELESS against EQUIPTact, which is the same pads on a soldier.
Strap and VELCRO handling adds a rustle bed. Velcro is itself very distinctive: a dense granular RIP with an extremely high onset rate and a broadband spectrum.
UCS's rule is about WHO IS WEARING IT: 'gear WORN while playing sports.' Balls, bats and rackets are not worn — those go to SPORTS or OBJECTS.
Against SPRTGym and OBJGym: the GAME versus the GEAR. If a player is USING it in play, prefer SPORTS.

EQUIPMENT is defined by USE, not by material or by morphology. Every subcategory here is the same physical event — a person handling worn or carried gear — producing a stochastic HANDLING TEXTURE: fabric rustle (broadband, high-centroid, noise-like) + leather creak (friction) + buckle and clasp jingle (sparse, bright, ringing impulses) + strap impacts. DSP can identify that texture precisely; it cannot tell whether the person wearing it is a soldier, an athlete, a hiker, or a horse. All six are honestly semantic_only.

WEBBING, BUCKLES AND METAL. Tactical gear is nylon webbing CARRYING HARD OBJECTS — magazines, clips, handcuffs, a holster, buckles — which makes it the RATTLIEST of the six: many bright metallic transients per second over a nylon rustle bed, each clink carrying a metallic ring (high inharmonicity).

One contextual regularity is worth encoding: tactical gear is nearly always heard while someone WALKS or RUNS, so the rattle is GAIT-MODULATED — a burst of jingle on every footfall, at ~1-2.5 Hz. That lifts onset_periodicity above the other EQUIPMENT subcategories, whose handling is usually static and stochastic. A periodic rattle at walking pace is a real cue.
Against EQUIPRec: recreational gear is nylon too, but carries far LESS metal — fewer ringing transients, more pure rustle. A real but soft lean.
Against EQUIPSprt: sport pads THUD; tactical gear CLINKS. Also real, also soft.

Against EQUIPBridle: acoustically the CLOSEST sibling of all — both are straps plus ringing metal, and they are genuinely inseparable by DSP. UCS splits them by WHO WEARS IT: a soldier or a horse. Pure text.
Ammo belts and bandoliers additionally overlap WEAPONS and GUNS handling.
